assignment.name: vague
assignment.title: Make something cool with AI
assignment.description: Build a project that uses AI in some interesting way. The exact scope, audience, and form of the deliverable are up to you.

advisory.reason: consultant budget exhausted; some leaves were not evaluated

verdict.notes: This plan fails on two fronts: it's a generic time-series forecasting project with no domain specificity or emergent complexity—the kind of tutorial application that teaches mechanics but not systems thinking—and the framing is so broad that it reads as 'I haven't decided what I'm building yet.' Stock price or traffic forecasting in 14 days will either be a shallow notebook exercise (ARIMA/LSTM on public data) or dangerously out of scope if you try to add real novelty. Anand expects you to commit to a concrete question and a modeling choice that creates conceptual richness, not a checklist of possible datasets.

verdict.notes: This plan fails on two fronts: it's a generic time-series forecasting project with no domain specificity or emergent complexity—the kind of tutorial application that teaches mechanics but not systems thinking—and the framing is so broad that it reads as 'I haven't decided what I'm building yet.' branches.option: Stock/crypto prices (financial markets)

history: Analytical/predictive (classification, forecasting, insights)
Time-series forecasting (stock prices, weather, traffic, etc.)
Stock/crypto prices (financial markets)

verdict.notes: This plan has no specificity—'time-series forecasting of stock prices' is a well-worn ML tutorial with negligible novelty, and the vague framing ('something cool') suggests the student hasn't committed to a real modeling question or domain insight. Even with 14 days, forecasting financial markets without a clear hypothesis, backtesting protocol, or responsible AI guardrails will produce either a generic sklearn pipeline or overfit noise; neither demonstrates systems thinking or intellectual rigor. The plan needs to anchor on a specific, defensible research question (e.g., 'Does sentiment analysis improve short-term volatility forecasting?') and commit to honest evaluation of failure modes.

branches.option: Weather patterns (temperature, precipitation)

history: Analytical/predictive (classification, forecasting, insights)
Time-series forecasting (stock prices, weather, traffic, etc.)
Weather patterns (temperature, precipitation)

verdict.notes: Weather forecasting is a well-trodden path with readily available datasets and pre-built models—it reads as a textbook exercise rather than something that demonstrates novel systems thinking or responsible AI reasoning. The plan lacks specificity on *which* weather patterns, *what* novel angle or stakeholder problem justifies the model, and *how* the student will avoid simply wrapping an existing forecast API. Even with 14 days, this needs a conceptual anchor (e.g., How does forecast uncertainty propagate into farmer decisions? How do competing stakeholders use conflicting forecasts?) to move beyond a data-science kata.

Anand expects you to define what question you're actually answering and for whom. The vagueness will lead to either a shallow notebook or analysis paralysis.

verdict.notes: This is a template, not a plan. You've listed three possible project *categories* with no commitment to a domain, dataset, problem statement, or deliverable form. Anand will ask: What are you actually building? Who is it for? What specific AI method solves a real problem here? Without these anchors, you cannot execute—you'll spend days deciding what to do instead of doing it. Pick one concrete application (e.g., 'anomaly detection in [specific domain] data via [specific method]') and scope it to 14 days.
